ai_strategy.summary: Indiana University Indianapolis's public AI posture combines published guidance, teaching resources, research centers, AI-relevant programs, and research computing. Public evidence points to distributed governance rather than a verified standalone chief AI officer.

key_provisions: Use appropriate or approved AI tools within institutional data rules
Faculty should set clear course-level AI expectations
Users remain responsible for accuracy, attribution, privacy, and academic integrity
Sensitive data should not be entered into unapproved public tools
ai_policy.summary: Indiana University provides system-level AI guidance through UITS and teaching resources, so IU Indianapolis inherits a coordinated framework covering tools, privacy, and pedagogy.

computing_infrastructure.summary: IU Indianapolis researchers can use IU system cyberinfrastructure including Big Red 200, a Cray EX AI supercomputer accelerated by 256 NVIDIA A100 Tensor Core GPUs and available to IU graduate students, faculty, staff, and sponsored undergraduates. The original Big Red 200 acquisition was reported as a $9.6M system funded by federal contracts and grants revenue.

regulatory_context.summary: Indiana University Indianapolis operates under federal education, privacy, and research obligations plus Indiana University system rules. This sweep found institutional AI guidance but no separate campus-specific AI statute.

narrative_summary: Indiana University Indianapolis is an R1 institution whose public AI posture is visible in responsible-use guidance, classroom resources, AI-relevant research structures, credentials or workforce programming, and research computing. The campus permits AI experimentation with guardrails rather than presenting a simple prohibition. Faculty-facing materials make course policy and assessment design central to implementation, while technology guidance emphasizes privacy, security, attribution, and responsible use. Research evidence comes from AI, machine-learning, data-science, policy, health, autonomy, or computing organizations, supported by high-performance or research computing resources. The profile is strongest on public policy resources, research centers, programs, and infrastructure, and weaker on exact budgets, enterprise license counts, adoption metrics, and named executive ownership. Overall, the institution appears to be in a growing-to-operational phase: AI work is real and distributed across functions, but public evidence does not yet show a single centralized AI operating model with disclosed outcomes.

Exact enterprise AI contract values, user counts, and adoption metrics were not publicly confirmed.
AI-specific total budget was not disclosed in the public sources used.
No institution-specific AI litigation or major controversy was confirmed.
IU discloses Big Red 200 GPU capacity and acquisition cost, but IU Indianapolis-specific AI platform user counts and AI-only operating budgets were not public.
